concepts.name: Pigeonhole Principle
concepts.intuition_gloss: If you stuff more pigeons than holes, some hole must hold at least two pigeons — an obvious fact with astonishing consequences.
concepts.formal_gloss: If n objects are placed into m boxes with n > m, then some box contains at least ceil(n/m) objects; formally, no injection exists from a larger finite set into a smaller one.

resources.title: MIT OCW 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science

sources: gateway existence argument in every combinatorics curriculum

concepts.name: Binomial Coefficients & Pascal's Triangle
concepts.intuition_gloss: The numbers in Pascal's triangle count how many ways to pick a handful from a pile, and they secretly run the expansion of (x+y)^n.
concepts.formal_gloss: C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!) counts k-subsets of an n-set, satisfies Pascal's recurrence C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1) + C(n-1,k), and gives (x+y)^n = sum of C(n,k) x^k y^(n-k).

resources.title: Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, 2nd ed., ch. 5 (Addison-Wesley)
Khan Academy: Combinations and the binomial theorem

concepts.name: Permutation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A shuffle of a finite set — every rearrangement decomposes into little loops that chase each other around.
concepts.formal_gloss: A bijection from a finite set to itself; the n! permutations of an n-set form the symmetric group S_n, and each factors uniquely into disjoint cycles.
sources: bridge object between counting and group theory

concepts.name: Inclusion–Exclusion Principle
concepts.intuition_gloss: To count things in overlapping groups, add the groups up, subtract the double-counted overlaps, add back the triple overlaps, and so on.
concepts.formal_gloss: |A_1 ∪ ... ∪ A_n| equals the alternating sum over nonempty index subsets S of (-1)^(|S|+1) |intersection of A_i for i in S|.

resources.title: MIT OCW 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science

sources: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications ch. 8

concepts.name: Bijective & Double-Counting Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: Prove two counts are equal by pairing the objects off one-to-one, or by counting the same pile from two different directions.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof that |A| = |B| by exhibiting an explicit bijection A → B, or that two expressions agree by showing both enumerate the same set.

resources.title: Benjamin & Quinn, Proofs that Really Count (MAA)

sources: Benjamin & Quinn (MAA, devoted entirely to the technique)
the signature proof style of enumerative combinatorics

concepts.name: Recurrence Relations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Define each term of a sequence from the ones before it — like Fibonacci's rabbits — then hunt for a closed-form shortcut.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation expressing a_n in terms of earlier terms; linear recurrences with constant coefficients are solved via characteristic roots or generating functions.

resources.title: Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, 2nd ed., ch. 1-2 (Addison-Wesley)

sources: Concrete Mathematics ch. 1 (Tower of Hanoi opener)
discrete mirror of differential equations — high leverage into CS

concepts.name: Generating Functions
concepts.intuition_gloss: A clothesline that hangs an entire infinite sequence on a single algebraic expression, so multiplying expressions does the counting for you.
concepts.formal_gloss: The formal power series A(x) = sum of a_n x^n encoding a sequence (a_n); products and functional operations on series correspond to combinatorial constructions on the counted objects.
sources: Wilf, generatingfunctionology ('a clothesline on which we hang up a sequence')

concepts.name: Integer Partitions & Compositions
concepts.intuition_gloss: The ways to break a whole number into a sum of smaller pieces — drawn as staircases of dots that reveal surprising symmetries.
concepts.formal_gloss: A partition of n is a weakly decreasing sequence of positive integers summing to n; p(n) counts them, with Euler's product formula giving the generating function 1/product(1-x^k).

resources.title: Andrews & Eriksson, Integer Partitions (Cambridge University Press)

sources: canonical meeting point of combinatorics and number theory (Euler, Ramanujan)

concepts.name: Paths, Cycles & Connectivity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whether and how you can travel through a network — which dots are reachable from which, and how many cuts it takes to break the network apart.
concepts.formal_gloss: A graph is connected if every vertex pair is joined by a path; k-connectivity measures the minimum vertices (or edges) whose removal disconnects it, with Menger's theorem equating this to disjoint-path counts.
sources: West, Introduction to Graph Theory ch. 4

concepts.name: Trees & Spanning Trees
concepts.intuition_gloss: A network with no loops — the leanest possible way to connect everything, from family trees to file systems.
concepts.formal_gloss: A connected acyclic graph on n vertices, equivalently a graph with exactly n-1 edges and a unique path between every pair; Cayley's formula counts n^(n-2) labeled trees on n vertices.
sources: ubiquitous data structure — high leverage into computation

concepts.name: Eulerian Circuits & Hamiltonian Cycles
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two famous tours of a network — trace every bridge exactly once (easy to check), or visit every city exactly once (famously hard).
concepts.formal_gloss: A connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree; deciding Hamiltonicity (a cycle through every vertex) is NP-complete, with only sufficient conditions like Dirac's theorem known.
sources: Euler 1736 (Königsberg — the founding problem of graph theory)

concepts.name: Planar Graphs & Euler's Formula
concepts.intuition_gloss: Networks you can draw on paper without wires crossing — and the magic count vertices minus edges plus faces always equals two.
concepts.formal_gloss: A graph embeddable in the plane with no edge crossings; connected planar graphs satisfy V - E + F = 2, and Kuratowski's theorem characterizes planarity by forbidden K5 and K3,3 subdivisions.

resources.title: Diestel, Graph Theory, ch. 4 (free online edition)

concepts.name: Graph Coloring
concepts.intuition_gloss: Paint the dots of a network so no wire joins two dots of the same color, using as few colors as possible — maps only ever need four.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proper k-coloring assigns k colors to vertices so adjacent vertices differ; the chromatic number χ(G) is the minimum such k, and the Four Color Theorem states χ ≤ 4 for planar graphs.
sources: Four Color Theorem (first major computer-assisted proof, 1976)

concepts.name: Matchings & Hall's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Pair up two groups — applicants to jobs, say — so nobody is doubled up; Hall's condition tells you exactly when everyone can be matched.
concepts.formal_gloss: A matching is a set of pairwise non-adjacent edges; Hall's theorem states a bipartite graph has a matching saturating side A iff every subset S of A has at least |S| neighbors.

resources.title: Diestel, Graph Theory, ch. 2 (free online edition)

sources: König's theorem / LP duality connection — Princeton Companion combinatorics chapters

concepts.name: Network Flows & Max-Flow Min-Cut
concepts.intuition_gloss: Push as much water as possible through a network of pipes — the bottleneck is always the tightest cut separating source from sink.
concepts.formal_gloss: In a capacitated directed graph, the maximum value of a source-to-sink flow equals the minimum capacity of an edge cut separating them (Ford-Fulkerson, 1956).

resources.title: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest & Stein, Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed., flow networks chapter (MIT Press)

sources: CLRS ch. 24 (flows; 4th ed. — ch. 26 in 3rd ed.)
unifies Menger, König, Hall — high structural leverage

concepts.name: Partially Ordered Sets & Lattices
concepts.intuition_gloss: Rankings where some pairs simply can't be compared — like tasks with prerequisites — drawn as layered diagrams of who-comes-before-whom.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with a reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive relation; chains and antichains structure it (Dilworth's theorem), and its Möbius function generalizes inclusion-exclusion.

concepts.name: Counting under Symmetry (Burnside & Pólya)
concepts.intuition_gloss: How many truly different necklaces can you bead, when rotating one doesn't make it new? Average the counts fixed by each symmetry.
concepts.formal_gloss: The number of orbits of a finite group G acting on a set equals the average number of fixed points of its elements; Pólya's theorem refines this with cycle-index generating functions.
sources: van Lint & Wilson, A Course in Combinatorics ch. 35
the canonical bridge from group theory into counting

concepts.name: Ramsey Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Complete disorder is impossible — any party of six people must contain three mutual friends or three mutual strangers.
concepts.formal_gloss: For all k, l there exists R(k,l) such that every 2-coloring of the edges of a complete graph on R(k,l) vertices contains a monochromatic K_k or K_l; generalizations pervade combinatorics.

resources.title: Graham, Rothschild & Spencer, Ramsey Theory, 2nd ed. (Wiley)

sources: R(5,5) still unknown — live research edge

concepts.name: Extremal Combinatorics
concepts.intuition_gloss: How big can a structure get before it's forced to contain a forbidden pattern — how many wires before a triangle must appear?
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of maximum or minimum sizes of set systems or graphs avoiding a given substructure; Turán's theorem gives the maximum edges in a K_r-free graph, with Szemerédi's regularity lemma as the deep modern tool.

resources.title: Diestel, Graph Theory, ch. 7 (free online edition)

concepts.name: The Probabilistic Method
concepts.intuition_gloss: To prove a needle exists in the haystack, show a random grab finds one with nonzero probability — no need to ever point at it.
concepts.formal_gloss: Prove an object with property P exists by showing a suitable random construction has P with positive probability; refinements include linearity of expectation, alteration, and the Lovász Local Lemma.

resources.title: Alon & Spencer, The Probabilistic Method, 4th ed. (Wiley)

sources: Erdős 1947 (Ramsey lower bound — the founding application)

concepts.name: Random Graphs
concepts.intuition_gloss: Flip a coin for every possible wire in a network and watch structure crystallize — a giant connected blob suddenly emerges at a sharp threshold.
concepts.formal_gloss: The G(n,p) model includes each of the C(n,2) edges independently with probability p; properties like connectivity appear at sharp thresholds, e.g. the giant component emerges at p ≈ 1/n.

resources.title: Bollobás, Random Graphs, 2nd ed. (Cambridge University Press)

sources: foundation of modern network science (territory tagline: 'networks everywhere')

concepts.name: Combinatorial Designs & Latin Squares
concepts.intuition_gloss: Arrange elements into groups so every pair meets equally often — the hidden math behind tournament schedules, sudoku, and error-correcting codes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A t-(v,k,λ) design is a family of k-subsets (blocks) of a v-set in which every t-subset lies in exactly λ blocks; Latin squares are n×n arrays with each symbol once per row and column.
sources: van Lint & Wilson, A Course in Combinatorics

concepts.name: Catalan Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: One sequence — 1, 1, 2, 5, 14, 42 — keeps turning up wherever you count balanced parentheses, mountain-shaped lattice paths, triangulated polygons, or binary trees.
concepts.formal_gloss: C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1) counts Dyck paths of length 2n, triangulations of an (n+2)-gon, binary trees with n internal nodes, and hundreds of other families; it satisfies the convolution recurrence C_(n+1) = sum of C_i C_(n-i) with generating function (1 - sqrt(1-4x))/(2x).

resources.title: Stanley, Enumerative Combinatorics (free online editions; Catalan interpretations in vol. 2 / the Catalan addendum)

sources: Stanley EC2 exercise 6.19 and the Catalan addendum
the most famous counted family in combinatorics

concepts.name: Set Partitions, Stirling & Bell Numbers
aka: Stirling numbers of the second kind
concepts.intuition_gloss: Count the ways to split a group of people into unlabeled teams — Stirling numbers count the splits into exactly k teams, Bell numbers count all possible splits at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set partition of an n-set is a family of disjoint nonempty blocks covering it (equivalently, an equivalence relation); S(n,k) counts partitions into k blocks via S(n,k) = k*S(n-1,k) + S(n-1,k-1), the Bell number B_n = sum over k of S(n,k), and the twelvefold way organizes balls-in-boxes counting by labeled/unlabeled and injective/surjective constraints.

resources.title: Stirling numbers of the second kind — Wikipedia

sources: companion to integer partitions: partitioning a set rather than a number
